ocr.text: The data presented in the table provides a comprehensive overview of microbial contamination levels on door handles,
highlighting the prevalence of various bacterial species. The findings indicate that Coagulase-negative Staphylococci (CoNS)
were the most frequently isolated organisms, with a total count of 52 samples detected on the surfaces examined. This
significant presence underscores the potential for these bacteria to contribute to nosocomial infections, particularly in healthcare

environments where door handles are frequently touched by staff and patients alike. 

In addition to CoNS, the table also reports
the isolation of Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, both of which were found in lower quantities, with two and one
sample respectively. The presence of E. coli is particularly concerning due to its association with gastrointestinal diseases,
highlighting the need for regular sanitation of high-touch surfaces.
Type and number of screened object  Type of organism detected  Total
CoNS  E. coli  Klebsiella  Proteus  P. aeruginosa  Serratia  S. aureus
Door handle (52)  2  1  1  2  3  -  4  13
Bed surface (34)  5  3  -  1  -  1  5  15
Stethoscope (20)  1  1  2  -  -  1  -  5
Thermometer (7)  3  -  1  1  -  -  1  6
Window handle (13)  -  3  2  -  -  1  -  6
Furthermore, the isolated P. aeruginosa, known for its resilience and pathogenicity, emphasizes the importance of maintaining
strict hygiene protocols to mitigate the risks of infection. Overall, these findings stress the necessity for enhanced cleaning
measures and monitoring protocols to reduce microbial load on frequently used surfaces.
